report_metadata.cancer_type: Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC) with MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutation

treatments.name: Surgical Resection (Lobectomy) + Adjuvant Cisplatin-Based Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple phase 3 RCTs and meta-analyses (LACE meta-analysis) establish surgical resection with adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy as standard of care for stage II NSCLC with 5.4% absolute 5-year OS improvement

survival_benefit.rationale: Stage II 5-year survival 46-60% with surgery + adjuvant chemo; curative intent approach with absolute survival benefit of 5.4% at 5 years from adjuvant chemotherapy; potentially curative for this 45-year-old ECOG 0 patient

accessibility.rationale: Universally available standard of care; lobectomy and cisplatin-based doublet widely accessible at all cancer centers

safety_profile.rationale: Surgical morbidity manageable in ECOG 0 patient; cisplatin-based chemotherapy has well-characterized toxicity profile including nephrotoxicity, nausea, and myelosuppression; hypertension and hyperlipidemia are manageable comorbidities

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery is biomarker-agnostic for resectable NSCLC; standard approach for stage II regardless of driver mutation; however adjuvant chemo benefit may be lower in MET-driven tumors due to relative chemoresistance

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Surgical lobectomy removes the primary tumor with curative intent. Adjuvant cisplatin-based doublet chemotherapy (typically cisplatin/pemetrexed for adenocarcinoma) eliminates micrometastatic disease to reduce recurrence risk.

treatments.name: Tepotinib (Tepmetko) — First-Line MET-Targeted Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: VISION phase 2 trial with 313 combined patients; FDA accelerated approval Feb 2021; treatment-naive ORR 57.3%, mPFS 11.2 months, mOS 19.6 months; 3+ year follow-up data confirms durable responses

survival_benefit.rationale: Treatment-naive ORR 57.3%, mPFS 11.2 months, mOS 19.6 months, exceptionally long mDOR 18.0 months; significantly outperforms chemotherapy for METex14 patients; durable responses maintained at 3+ year follow-up

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (accelerated) for METex14 NSCLC (Feb 2021); NCCN Category 2A preferred recommendation; widely available in US, EU, Japan; once-daily oral dosing

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than capmatinib overall; peripheral edema is main toxicity (up to 63% all grade, lower grade 3+); once-daily 450mg convenient dosing; monitor for interstitial lung disease (rare); manageable in 45-year-old ECOG 0

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets MET exon 14 skipping mutation; highly selective Type Ib MET inhibitor; excellent match for this patient's METex14 plus MET amplification profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tepotinib is a highly selective, once-daily oral Type Ib MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor. It binds to the ATP-binding site of the MET kinase domain, blocking autophosphorylation and downstream signaling through RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR cascades, inhibiting tumor proliferation, survival, and migration driven by aberrant MET activation.

treatments.name: Capmatinib (Tabrecta) — First-Line MET-Targeted Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: GEOMETRY mono-1 phase 2 trial with 160 METex14 patients; FDA accelerated approval 2020, converted to full approval; treatment-naive ORR 68%, mPFS 12.5 months, mOS 21.4 months; single-arm but robust data

survival_benefit.rationale: Treatment-naive ORR 68%, mPFS 12.5 months, median OS 21.4 months, mDOR 16.6 months; significantly superior to chemotherapy (ORR 25-27%) for METex14 patients; provides systemic disease control for recurrence after surgery

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for METex14 NSCLC (May 2020 accelerated, subsequently converted to regular approval); NCCN Category 2A preferred recommendation; widely available in US, EU, Japan

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 AEs in 73%; peripheral edema 17% grade 3+; increased lipase 9%; treatment discontinuation 16%; hypertension requires monitoring given patient's pre-existing HTN; manageable but notable toxicity burden

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets MET exon 14 skipping mutation, the patient's confirmed actionable driver; also addresses concurrent MET amplification; perfectly matched to this patient's molecular profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Capmatinib is a highly selective, potent Type Ib MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor that blocks MET-dependent signaling. In MET exon 14 skipping mutations, the MET receptor lacks the exon 14-encoded juxtamembrane domain containing the CBL E3 ubiquitin ligase binding site, leading to decreased MET degradation and sustained oncogenic signaling. Capmatinib inhibits MET phosphorylation and downstream RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and STAT3 pathways.

treatments.name: Amivantamab (Rybrevant) — EGFR/MET Bispecific Antibody

evidence_level.rationale: CHRYSALIS phase 1 trial with 97 METex14 patients; final results published 2025; ORR 32% overall, 50% in treatment-naive, 46% in MET-TKI naive; FDA approved for EGFR-mutant NSCLC but studied in METex14 as well

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 50% in treatment-naive METex14 patients; meaningful activity even after prior MET TKI (ORR 19%); provides important option after TKI resistance; however ORR lower than capmatinib/tepotinib in frontline

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for EGFR-mutant NSCLC; for METex14 indication, off-label or clinical trial access; IV infusion (not oral); available at major cancer centers

safety_profile.rationale: Infusion-related reactions in ~65% of patients (mostly grade 1-2 with first dose); skin rash (EGFR-mediated); hypoalbuminemia; IV administration requires infusion center visits

biomarker_match.rationale: Bispecific antibody targeting both EGFR and MET; addresses MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification simultaneously; unique mechanism complements TKI approach; also has immune cell-directing activity

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Amivantamab is a fully human EGFR-MET bispecific antibody with immune cell-directing activity. It binds both EGFR and MET extracellular domains simultaneously, blocking ligand binding, inducing receptor degradation, and mediating antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and trogocytosis via Fc-mediated immune effector functions. This distinct mechanism can overcome on-target kinase domain mutations that cause resistance to MET TKIs.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (EGFR-mutant NSCLC); Off-label/Clinical Trial for METex14

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Platinum-Based Chemotherapy (Immunochemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-189 phase 3 RCT established pembrolizumab + pemetrexed/platinum as standard first-line for nonsquamous NSCLC; however METex14 patients were not specifically studied and represent a small subgroup

survival_benefit.rationale: METex14 patients show lower response to ICI-based therapy: ORR 33% with chemo-IO vs 50-68% with MET TKIs; retrospective data suggest attenuated ICI benefit despite high PD-L1; however 2025 data from select patients show mOS 45.2 months suggesting some benefit

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved standard of care for nonsquamous NSCLC; universally available; no molecular testing requirement for access (though MET-targeted therapy preferred when METex14 detected)

safety_profile.rationale: Chemotherapy toxicity (myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, nausea) plus immune-related adverse events; risk of pneumonitis relevant in lung cancer; hypertension monitoring needed given patient's comorbidity

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-specific for METex14; METex14 tumors often have high PD-L1 but low TMB, leading to variable ICI response (ORR 14-33%); NCCN prefers MET-targeted therapy over immunochemotherapy for METex14 patients

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, reactivating T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. Combined with platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy for cytotoxic tumor cell killing and immunogenic cell death, which may enhance immune response. METex14 tumors frequently overexpress PD-L1 but have low tumor mutational burden, creating an uncertain immunotherapy benefit profile.

treatments.name: Vebreltinib — Next-Generation MET TKI

evidence_level.rationale: KUNPENG phase 2 trial with 52 METex14 patients; ORR 75%, mPFS 14.1 months, mOS 20.7 months, mDOR 15.9 months; approved in China (June 2025); excellent brain metastasis activity (100% ORR)

survival_benefit.rationale: Highest reported ORR (75%) among MET TKIs for METex14 NSCLC; mPFS 14.1 months and mOS 20.7 months compare favorably to capmatinib and tepotinib; mDOR 15.9 months indicates durable responses

accessibility.rationale: Approved in China (NMPA, June 2025) for METex14 and MET-amplified NSCLC; not available in US/EU outside clinical trials; limited global access currently

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated; adverse events similar to other Type Ib MET TKIs including peripheral edema and GI effects; manageable toxicity profile

biomarker_match.rationale: Type Ib MET TKI targeting MET exon 14 skipping mutation and MET amplification; directly matches patient's dual MET alterations

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vebreltinib is a potent, selective Type Ib MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor that targets the ATP-binding site of MET kinase. It demonstrates excellent CNS penetration and potent activity against MET exon 14 skipping mutations and MET amplification, with particular efficacy in brain metastasis settings.
treatments.availability: NMPA Approved (China, June 2025); Clinical Trial access elsewhere

treatments.name: Glumetinib (Gumarontinib) — Selective MET TKI

evidence_level.rationale: GLORY phase 1b/2 trial with 79 evaluable METex14 patients; ORR 66%, mPFS 8.5 months; NMPA approved in China (March 2023); notable for 85% ORR in patients with brain metastases

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 66% overall (71% treatment-naive); mPFS 8.5 months; excellent brain metastasis activity (85% ORR); mDOR 8.3 months; competitive efficacy among MET TKIs

accessibility.rationale: Approved only in China (NMPA, March 2023); not available in US/EU outside clinical trials; limited global access

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated with manageable adverse events; peripheral edema and GI toxicities are primary concerns; safety profile similar to other Type Ib MET TKIs

biomarker_match.rationale: Selective MET TKI directly targeting MET exon 14 skipping mutation; strong biomarker match for this patient's molecular profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Glumetinib (gumarontinib) is a potent, selective Type Ib MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor that blocks MET kinase activity by binding to the ATP-binding site. It demonstrates excellent intracranial penetration and is particularly effective against MET exon 14 skipping mutations, with notable activity in brain metastasis settings (85% ORR).

treatments.name: Savolitinib (Orpathys) — Selective MET Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2/3b trials with 70 METex14 patients; NMPA full approval in China (Jan 2025); ORR 42.9% overall, treatment-naive 46.4%; mPFS 6.8 months, mOS 12.5 months; not FDA-approved as monotherapy

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 42.9%, mPFS 6.8 months, mOS 12.5 months; efficacy lower than capmatinib/tepotinib in head-to-head comparison of trial results; however useful option especially in combination with osimertinib

accessibility.rationale: Approved in China (NMPA full approval Jan 2025) for METex14 NSCLC; not FDA-approved as monotherapy; available in clinical trials globally; limited availability in US/EU

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated; main toxicities include peripheral edema, nausea, and liver enzyme elevations; lower grade 3+ toxicity rate compared to capmatinib; manageable profile for 45-year-old patient

biomarker_match.rationale: Selective MET inhibitor directly targeting the patient's MET exon 14 skipping mutation and concurrent MET amplification; strong biomarker match

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Savolitinib is a highly selective Type Ib MET tyrosine kinase inhibitor that binds to the ATP-binding pocket of the MET kinase domain. It potently inhibits MET phosphorylation driven by MET exon 14 skipping mutations and MET amplification, blocking downstream oncogenic signaling through the RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways.
treatments.availability: NMPA Approved (China); Clinical Trial access globally

treatments.name: Telisotuzumab Vedotin (Emrelis) — Anti-MET Antibody-Drug Conjugate

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 LUMINOSITY trial; FDA accelerated approval May 2025 for c-MET overexpressing NSCLC; ORR 35% in high c-MET patients; first-in-class MET-directed ADC; novel mechanism addresses MET overexpression beyond exon 14 skipping

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 35% with mDOR 7.2 months in previously treated patients with high c-MET overexpression; indicated after prior systemic therapy; provides a subsequent-line option when MET TKIs fail

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval May 2025; available at major cancer centers; IV infusion every 2 weeks; companion diagnostic required for c-MET overexpression testing

safety_profile.rationale: ADC-related toxicities including peripheral neuropathy, nausea, fatigue; ocular toxicity reported; infusion-related reactions; manageable but requires monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has MET amplification which typically leads to high c-MET protein overexpression; requires IHC confirmation of high c-MET (3+ in >=50% cells); different biomarker requirement than MET TKIs but relevant to this patient's profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Telisotuzumab vedotin is a first-in-class c-MET-directed antibody-drug conjugate (ADC). It comprises an anti-c-MET antibody conjugated to monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE) via a cleavable linker. Upon binding to c-MET on tumor cells, the ADC is internalized and MMAE is released intracellularly, causing microtubule disruption and apoptosis. This mechanism is distinct from kinase inhibition and can address tumors with MET overexpression regardless of mutation status.
biomarker_requirements: High c-MET protein overexpression (IHC 3+ in >=50% tumor cells)

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant Capmatinib (GEOMETRY-N Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 GEOMETRY-N trial (NCT04926831) ongoing; evaluating neoadjuvant + adjuvant capmatinib in resectable stage IB-IIIA METex14 NSCLC; case reports show pathologic complete response with neoadjuvant MET TKI; no mature efficacy data yet

survival_benefit.rationale: Extrapolating from 68% ORR in advanced disease, neoadjuvant approach could achieve tumor downstaging and improve surgical outcomes; case reports of pathologic complete response with neoadjuvant crizotinib/savolitinib for METex14; high potential for this stage II patient

accessibility.rationale: Available through GEOMETRY-N clinical trial (NCT04926831) at select centers; capmatinib itself is FDA-approved but perioperative use is investigational; requires trial enrollment

safety_profile.rationale: Capmatinib safety profile well-characterized; perioperative use adds surgical risk considerations but 45-year-old ECOG 0 patient is ideal surgical candidate; edema may need management perioperatively

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets the patient's confirmed MET exon 14 skipping mutation in the neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting; rational use of a biomarker-matched targeted therapy perioperatively

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant capmatinib is administered before surgery to shrink the METex14-driven tumor, potentially achieving pathologic response and making surgery more effective. Adjuvant capmatinib after surgery targets residual micrometastatic disease harboring MET exon 14 skipping mutation, analogous to the paradigm established for EGFR TKIs (osimertinib) in the adjuvant setting.

treatments.name: Crizotinib — Multi-Kinase Inhibitor (ALK/ROS1/MET)

evidence_level.rationale: PROFILE 1001 trial with 65 evaluable METex14 patients; ORR 32%, mPFS 7.3 months, mDOR 9.1 months; FDA approved for ALK/ROS1 NSCLC; off-label use for METex14 supported by evidence but inferior to selective MET TKIs

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 32% significantly lower than capmatinib (68%) and tepotinib (57%) in treatment-naive patients; mPFS 7.3 months; serves as proof-of-concept for MET inhibition but outperformed by selective inhibitors

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved (for ALK/ROS1); widely available; can be used off-label for METex14; generic versions available; not NCCN preferred for METex14 given availability of capmatinib/tepotinib

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity: visual disturbances, nausea, diarrhea, peripheral edema, hepatotoxicity; QTc prolongation requires monitoring; less selective than newer MET TKIs leading to more off-target effects

biomarker_match.rationale: Multi-kinase inhibitor with MET activity; Type Ia MET inhibitor; less potent and selective than capmatinib/tepotinib for METex14; may cover concurrent ALK or ROS1 alterations if present

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Crizotinib is a first-generation multi-kinase inhibitor targeting ALK, ROS1, and MET (Type Ia). It inhibits MET kinase activity and downstream signaling but with lower selectivity than dedicated MET TKIs. Its Type Ia binding mode differs from Type Ib inhibitors and may retain activity against some Type Ib-resistant mutations.

treatments.name: Ensartinib — Repurposed ALK/MET Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: EMBRACE phase 2 trial with 31 METex14 patients; ORR 53.3%, DCR 86.7% at median 9.2 months follow-up; classified as Type Ia MET inhibitor; published in eClinicalMedicine 2025

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 53.3% with DCR 86.7%; ctDNA clearance correlates with improved outcomes (ORR 75% in ctDNA clearers vs 16.7%); however limited follow-up and smaller sample size than established MET TKIs

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trials; not FDA-approved for METex14 indication; originally developed as ALK inhibitor; limited availability outside trials

safety_profile.rationale: Differentiated safety profile with lower incidence of peripheral edema compared to Type Ib MET TKIs; well-tolerated in elderly patients; favorable for this 45-year-old patient

biomarker_match.rationale: Type Ia MET inhibitor effective against MET exon 14 skipping mutations; ctDNA monitoring enables early response assessment; different binding mode from Type Ib inhibitors may provide option after Type Ib resistance

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ensartinib is a second-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor primarily targeting ALK but also functioning as a potent Type Ia MET inhibitor. Unlike Type Ib inhibitors (capmatinib, tepotinib), ensartinib binds to a different region of the MET kinase domain, potentially offering activity against some Type Ib-resistant mutations. It inhibits MET phosphorylation and downstream signaling in METex14-driven tumors.
notable_side_effects: Lower incidence of peripheral edema vs Type Ib MET TKIs

treatments.name: Capmatinib + Spartalizumab (anti-PD-1) Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Randomized phase 2 trial (NCT04323436) evaluating capmatinib + spartalizumab vs capmatinib + placebo as first-line METex14 NSCLC; results pending; rationale based on potential synergy between MET inhibition and immune checkpoint blockade

survival_benefit.rationale: Rationale: METex14 tumors often express high PD-L1 and immune checkpoint inhibition may add benefit to MET TKI; recent 2025 data showed mPFS 28.8 months and mOS 45.2 months in patients who received ICI at some point; combining could improve outcomes over TKI alone

accessibility.rationale: Available through clinical trial NCT04323436; spartalizumab not FDA-approved individually; trial enrollment required at participating centers

safety_profile.rationale: Combination of MET TKI toxicity (edema, nausea) with potential immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies); increased toxicity risk but potentially manageable in 45-year-old ECOG 0 patient

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets MET exon 14 skipping with capmatinib; anti-PD-1 leverages high PD-L1 expression commonly seen in METex14 tumors; dual mechanism addresses the patient's molecular profile

treatments.mechanism_of_action: This combination pairs capmatinib (selective MET TKI) with spartalizumab (anti-PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor). Capmatinib directly inhibits MET-driven oncogenic signaling while spartalizumab unleashes anti-tumor T cell immunity. METex14 tumors frequently express high PD-L1, providing rationale for immune checkpoint blockade. The combination aims to achieve synergistic tumor control through simultaneous targeting of tumor-intrinsic oncogenic signaling and immune evasion.

treatments.name: Tepotinib + Ramucirumab Combination (Lung-MAP Sub-Study)

evidence_level.rationale: Randomized phase 2 Lung-MAP sub-study evaluating tepotinib with or without ramucirumab; early stage investigation; rationale based on anti-angiogenic synergy and potential to reduce tepotinib-associated peripheral edema

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical rationale for synergy between MET inhibition and anti-VEGFR2 therapy; ramucirumab may reduce peripheral edema (anti-VEGF effect); no mature efficacy data available yet

accessibility.rationale: Available through Lung-MAP clinical trial at SWOG network sites; tepotinib is FDA-approved and ramucirumab is FDA-approved for other indications; combination investigational

safety_profile.rationale: Combination adds anti-VEGFR2 toxicities (bleeding, hypertension, proteinuria) to MET TKI side effects; patient's pre-existing hypertension requires careful monitoring with ramucirumab

biomarker_match.rationale: Tepotinib directly targets MET exon 14 skipping mutation; ramucirumab adds anti-angiogenic activity; dual targeting of MET-driven and VEGF-driven pathways

treatments.mechanism_of_action: This combination pairs tepotinib (selective MET TKI) with ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2 monoclonal antibody). Tepotinib inhibits MET-driven oncogenic signaling while ramucirumab blocks VEGF-mediated angiogenesis, reducing tumor blood supply. The anti-VEGF activity of ramucirumab may also reduce peripheral edema, a common MET TKI side effect, while providing additive anti-tumor activity.

treatments.name: Telisotuzumab Adizutecan (ABBV-400) — Next-Gen MET-Directed ADC

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 trial data reported at ASCO 2025; early clinical evidence in EGFR-mutated NSCLC with MET alterations; promising activity as next-generation MET-directed ADC with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload

survival_benefit.rationale: Early phase 1 data show promising clinical activity; novel topoisomerase I payload (adizutecan) may be more potent than MMAE payload of telisotuzumab vedotin; potential to address broader MET-altered population

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial only; not yet FDA-approved; available through AbbVie-sponsored trials at select sites; likely 2-3 years from potential approval

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable safety profile reported in phase 1; topoisomerase I inhibitor payload may cause GI toxicities and myelosuppression; interstitial lung disease risk requires monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets c-MET protein overexpression; patient's MET amplification likely results in high c-MET expression; ADC mechanism bypasses kinase domain resistance mutations

treatments.mechanism_of_action: ABBV-400 (telisotuzumab adizutecan) is a next-generation c-MET-directed antibody-drug conjugate carrying a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (adizutecan) instead of the MMAE payload used in telisotuzumab vedotin. This ADC binds c-MET on tumor cells, is internalized, and releases the topoisomerase I inhibitor to cause DNA damage and cell death. The bystander effect may also kill neighboring tumor cells with lower MET expression.

clinical_trials.title: GEOMETRY-N: Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Capmatinib in Resectable NSCLC With MET Exon 14 Skipping or High MET Amplification
clinical_trials.early_results: No mature data available; evaluating major pathological response rate with neoadjuvant capmatinib in resectable stage IB-IIIA METex14 NSCLC

clinical_trials.title: Study of Capmatinib and Spartalizumab/Placebo in Advanced NSCLC Patients With MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutations
clinical_trials.early_results: Randomized study evaluating capmatinib + spartalizumab (anti-PD-1) vs capmatinib + placebo as first-line treatment; results anticipated

clinical_trials.title: APL-101 Study in Subjects With NSCLC With c-Met Exon 14 Skip Mutations and c-Met Dysregulation Advanced Solid Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Multiple cohorts including treatment-naive (Cohort A-1), previously treated (Cohort A-2), and MET inhibitor-experienced (Cohort B) patients; enrollment ongoing

clinical_trials.title: Randomized Phase II Study of Tepotinib With or Without Ramucirumab in MET Exon 14 Skipping Positive Stage IV or Recurrent NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating whether adding ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR2) to tepotinib improves efficacy and reduces peripheral edema; no mature results yet

clinical_trials.title: CHRYSALIS: A Study of Amivantamab in Participants With Advanced NSCLC Including MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutations
clinical_trials.early_results: Final results (2025): ORR 32% overall, 50% treatment-naive, 46% MET-TKI naive, 19% post-MET-TKI; clinically meaningful durable responses including post-TKI resistance

clinical_trials.title: Telisotuzumab Adizutecan (ABBV-400) in Advanced NSCLC With MET Alterations
clinical_trials.early_results: Next-generation MET-directed ADC with topoisomerase I inhibitor payload; phase 1 data presented at ASCO 2025 showing promising activity in MET-altered NSCLC

clinical_trials.title: Ensartinib for Advanced or Metastatic NSCLC With MET Exon 14 Skipping Mutations
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 53.3%, DCR 86.7% at median 9.2 months follow-up; ctDNA clearance at 4 weeks correlates with improved outcomes (ORR 75% in clearers vs 16.7%)

combination_strategies.rationale: METex14 tumors frequently express high PD-L1; combining MET inhibition with immune checkpoint blockade may achieve synergistic anti-tumor activity by simultaneously targeting oncogenic signaling and immune evasion; high PD-L1 expression in METex14 tumors provides biological rationale

Anti-VEGFR2 therapy may synergize with MET inhibition through anti-angiogenic effects; ramucirumab may also reduce peripheral edema (most common MET TKI side effect) through VEGF pathway modulation; dual targeting of MET and VEGF signaling pathways

Neoadjuvant capmatinib can shrink METex14-driven tumors before surgery, potentially improving resection margins and pathologic response; adjuvant capmatinib targets residual micrometastatic disease; parallels successful EGFR TKI adjuvant paradigm (ADAURA trial for osimertinib)

Amivantamab's antibody-based mechanism of action (receptor degradation, ADCC, trogocytosis) is orthogonal to TKI kinase inhibition; can overcome on-target kinase domain resistance mutations (D1228, Y1230) that render Type Ib MET TKIs ineffective; provides meaningful activity post-TKI (ORR 19%)

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Type Ia MET TKI (Crizotinib or Ensartinib)
combination_strategies.rationale: Type Ia MET inhibitors bind a different region of the MET kinase domain than Type Ib inhibitors; switching from Type Ib (capmatinib/tepotinib) to Type Ia (crizotinib/ensartinib) may overcome specific resistance mutations; D1228 mutations confer resistance to Type Ib but retain sensitivity to Type Ia inhibitors

supportive_care.evidence: Temel et al. (NEJM 2010) randomized trial of 151 metastatic NSCLC patients showed early palliative care improved quality of life (FACT-L score 98.0 vs 91.5, p=0.03) and overall survival (11.6 vs 8.9 months, p=0.02). PACO trial confirmed benefits in additional NSCLC settings.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved quality of life, symptom control, mood, and overall survival (median OS improvement of 2.7 months); reduced aggressive end-of-life interventions; particularly important for this young patient to maintain quality of life during treatment

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple meta-analyses demonstrate aerobic and resistance exercise during and after cancer treatment improves cardiorespiratory fitness, physical function, fatigue, and quality of life. NCCN Survivorship Guidelines v2.2025 recommend physical activity for all cancer patients. Particular benefit for managing MET TKI-related fatigue and peripheral edema.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved cardiorespiratory fitness, reduced cancer-related fatigue, better tolerance of systemic therapy, reduced peripheral edema (relevant for MET TKI side effect), improved mood and anxiety; this 45-year-old ECOG 0 patient is ideal candidate for intensive exercise programs

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Guidelines recommend nutritional screening and counseling for all lung cancer patients. Adequate protein and caloric intake supports surgical recovery (relevant for stage II resection) and systemic therapy tolerance. Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns may complement anti-cancer therapy.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved surgical outcomes, better tolerance of adjuvant chemotherapy and/or MET TKI therapy, maintained body composition, improved quality of life; important for managing GI side effects of MET TKIs and chemotherapy

supportive_care.evidence: Patient has pre-existing hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Several MET TKIs can exacerbate peripheral edema and fluid retention. Cardio-oncology guidelines recommend proactive blood pressure and lipid management during targeted therapy. Ramucirumab combinations require hypertension monitoring.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevention of cardiovascular complications during cancer therapy; optimized blood pressure control reduces risk of MET TKI-associated edema and enables combination strategies; statin use may have additional anti-cancer properties (observational data in NSCLC)

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management Guidelines recommend psychosocial screening for all cancer patients. Young patient (45 years) with cancer diagnosis may experience significant psychological impact. Cognitive behavioral therapy and support groups improve adjustment and quality of life.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced anxiety and depression, improved treatment adherence, better quality of life, enhanced coping strategies; particularly important for this young patient facing a cancer diagnosis at 45 years of age

supportive_care.evidence: Peripheral edema is the most common side effect of MET TKIs (up to 63% with tepotinib, 51% with capmatinib). Evidence-based management includes compression stockings, leg elevation, salt restriction, and dose modification if grade 3+. Diuretics may be considered for symptomatic relief.
supportive_care.benefit: Enables continued MET TKI therapy at full dose; improves quality of life; prevents dose reductions that may compromise efficacy; proactive management is more effective than reactive treatment
